title: NIS2 Asset Management and Configuration Baseline
exclusions: Does not auto-quarantine hosts, approve firewall changes, replace official CMDB reconciliation ceremonies, or satisfy hardware disposal evidence outside Splunk’s scope.
evidence: Daily `UC-22.2.13` result set, ES screenshot of enriched assets, ServiceNow `cmdb_ci` change references for remediated IPs, and RFC3161-sealed CSV via `evidenceSigning` each quarter.

compliance.assurance_rationale: Art.21(2)(i) requires asset management alongside access control. The UC continuously reconciles discovered assets against the authorised CMDB baseline to flag unauthorised or drifted configurations. Policy definition and lifecycle governance remain outside Splunk, hence partial.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that NIS2 Art.21(2)(i) (Human resources and access control) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.2.13: NIS2 Asset Management and Configuration Baseline.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.2.13' running on index vulnerability and supporting catalogue-defined data sources, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

controlTest.positiveScenario: Test VLAN generates 12 TB/day from IP `10.66.7.8` with no `asset_lookup_by_str` row; within first hourly run the correlation search lists the IP in the top position with `category=unknown`, satisfying the detective control.
controlTest.negativeScenario: After CMDB sync adds the same IP with `category=production_db` and `owner=dba_team`, and `nis2_asset_exception_register.csv` documents the disaster-recovery floating address, rerunning the search yields zero rows post-filters despite continued high volume.

dataSources: Splunk Enterprise Security asset lookups (`asset_lookup_by_str`, fields category, priority, owner, nt_host) plus `index=cmdb` `sourcetype=snow:cmdb_ci_server` (fields sys_id, host_name, u_business_service) via Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow ([Splunkbase 1928](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/1928)); accelerated CIM `Network_Traffic.All_Traffic` with `All_Traffic.src`, `All_Traffic.dest`, `All_Traffic.bytes`.
app: Splunk Enterprise Security (Splunkbase 263), Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow (Splunkbase 1928), Splunk Add-on for Qualys (Splunkbase 2964), Splunk Common Information Model Add-on (Splunkbase 1621)
spl:
| tstats `summariesonly` dc(All_Traffic.dest_port) as ports, sum(All_Traffic.bytes) as total_bytes
  from datamodel=Network_Traffic.All_Traffic
  by All_Traffic.src
| rename All_Traffic.src as src
| lookup asset_lookup_by_str key AS src OUTPUT category, priority, owner, nt_host
| where isnull(category) OR category="unknown"
| sort - total_bytes
| head 100
| table src, nt_host, category, owner, ports, total_bytes
description: Uses fifteen-minute (or better) summarized east-west traffic from the accelerated Network Traffic model and compares each internal `src` IP against the ES asset lookup to expose high-volume senders that lack an approved CMDB category.
value: NIS2 Article 21 expects inventories that stay trustworthy between annual audits; continuously proving that mystery IPs do not dominate traffic gives assurance that change windows, segmentation projects, and CMDB hygiene actually stick—not just spreadsheets.
implementation: (1) Keep `asset_lookup_by_str` synced from ServiceNow `cmdb_ci_server` nightly. (2) Accelerate CIM Network_Traffic for 30+ days. (3) Run search daily; route top `/16` hits to network engineering. (4) Join `uc-22.2.9` dashboard KPIs. (5) Reference `nis2_asset_exception_register.csv` for lab VLANs.
visualization: Table of unknown senders, choropleth if geo IP enabled, link to Qualys host detail.
cimSpl: | tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Network_Traffic.All_Traffic by All_Traffic.src, All_Traffic.dest, All_Traffic.dest_port | sort - count
knownFalsePositives: 1) **Qualys passive scanner appliances** appearing as top talkers. *Distinguish:* `src` matches `qualys_scanner_subnets.csv`. *Suppress:* `lookup qualys_scanner_subnets subnet AS src OUTPUT-ignore=true | where isnull(ignore)`. 2) **ServiceNow MID Server NAT pools** sharing one external IP. *Distinguish:* `owner=MID_Server_*` in CMDB. *Suppress:* enrich via `lookup mid_nat_map`. 3) **Kubernetes node masquerade traffic** where pod IP not in CMDB. *Distinguish:* `src` inside `10.42.0.0/16` overlay. *Suppress:* time-bound exception for cluster builds in `nis2_asset_exception_register.csv`. 4) **Ephemeral AWS Lambda egress** missing static CMDB rows. *Distinguish:* flow logs include `eni-*` interface tag. *Suppress:* join `index=aws sourcetype=aws:cloudwatchlogs:vpcflow`. 5) **Guest wireless VLAN** allowed to be `unknown`. *Distinguish:* `src` in `guest_wifi_supernet`. *Suppress:* `where NOT cidrmatch("10.200.0.0/16",src)`.
equipmentModels: Splunk Add-on for Qualys (Splunkbase 2964, optional host detail enrichment)
Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow (Splunkbase 1928, CMDB CI server table)
Splunk CIM Add-on (Splunkbase 1621, Network_Traffic data model acceleration)
Splunk Enterprise Security 7.x (Splunkbase 263, asset_lookup_by_str for CMDB enrichment)
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Lookups**: `asset_lookup_by_str` must be deployed to the search-tier app and refreshed on a documented schedule. For KV Store lookups, verify with `| rest /servicesNS/-/-/data/transforms/lookups | search name IN ('asset_lookup_by_str')`.
• **Data Model Acceleration**: The CIM datamodel referenced in the search must be accelerated. Validate with `| rest /services/admin/summarization | search summary.id=*` and confirm `summary.complete=100`.
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `Art.21(2)(i)`. The control objective is: "Evidence that NIS2 Art.21(2)(i) (Human resources and access control) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.2.13: NIS2 Asset Management and Configuration Baseline." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is bespoke for this UC — no single canonical TA parses every source the detection relies on. Route data into the relevant indexes under the relevant sourcetypes, and document the ingestion path in the UC runbook so future engineers know where each field originates.


5 operational considerations apply to the rollout of this detection:


• **Hint 1:** Keep `asset_lookup_by_str` synced from ServiceNow `cmdb_ci_server` nightly. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 2:** Accelerate CIM Network_Traffic for 30+ days. CIM-compliance tagging must be validated end-to-end: a downstream datamodel that does not accelerate because of a missing tag is a silent failure.

• **Hint 3:** Run search daily; route top `/16` hits to network engineering. Routing targets (SOC, compliance team, specific queue) must be documented with named on-call owners; an 'alerts go to the team' runbook fails at 3am on Sunday.

• **Hint 4:** Join `uc-22.2.9` dashboard KPIs. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 5:** Reference `nis2_asset_exception_register.csv` for lab VLANs. The register referenced here is primary evidence — treat changes to it with the same discipline as code changes.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

For "NIS2 Asset Management and Configuration Baseline," the aggregation produces the result shape required by the NIS2 control — each row is a summary record the regulation expects to be presentable at audit time.

This search uses `| tstats` against a CIM-accelerated data model rather than reading raw events. The performance delta is typically 10-50x over raw search, and for large indexes can be >100x. The acceleration summaries are maintained on a rolling window (default: 1 year, configurable in the data model editor) and are queried from indexer-local summary files rather than re-scanning raw events.

CIM acceleration dependency: this search FAILS SILENTLY if the underlying data model is not accelerated. It will return zero rows instead of raising an error, which is exactly the wrong failure mode for a compliance detection. Verify acceleration status before scheduling:

```spl
| rest /services/admin/summarization
| search title="<datamodel_name>"
| table title access_count summary.complete summary.earliest_time summary.latest_time
```

`summary.complete` should show 100 (or very close). If it's below 95, the acceleration is still catching up or has stalled — check the DMA audit log (`index=_audit action=dma_run`) for errors.

Aggregation choice: `dc()` is grouped by `All_Traffic.src`. This produces a summary row per distinct combination, which is typically what compliance reporting requires (one row per asset, one row per user, one row per rule). If downstream processing expects a flat event stream, avoid `tstats` — summary rows are not raw events and cannot be drilled into without re-running against the raw index.

When NOT to use tstats: if the field being filtered was added after the acceleration started, or is search-time extracted by an app the acceleration isn't aware of, the field will not exist in the summaries and the `where` clause will drop every row. Rebuild the acceleration after any field-extraction changes.

**CIM / accelerated variant:** The detection also has an accelerated variant using `| tstats` against the CIM `Network_Traffic` datamodel:

```spl
| tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Network_Traffic.All_Traffic by All_Traffic.src, All_Traffic.dest, All_Traffic.dest_port | sort - count
```

When to prefer this variant: once the underlying datamodel is accelerated (verify in Settings > Data Models > `Network_Traffic` > status=100%), this query runs 10-50x faster than the raw variant and can feasibly cover multi-month windows for audit reporting. When to avoid: if your field extractions are app-specific and the CIM tagging is incomplete, the `tstats` variant will silently under-count. Always compare raw vs accelerated counts on a known day before swapping the production schedule. Maintain both variants in the UC — the raw is the primary for accuracy, the accelerated is the performance optimisation for dashboarding.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.2.13: NIS2 Asset Management and Configuration Baseline]
cron_schedule = 0 */6 * * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -24h@h
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.2.13,reg=NIS2
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=the_index sourcetype=the_sourcetype earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `category`, `nt_host`, `owner`, `ports`, `src`, `total_bytes`. Run:

```spl
index=the_index sourcetype=the_sourcetype earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table category, nt_host, owner, ports, src, total_bytes
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(c) Lookup freshness:** The detection joins against `asset_lookup_by_str`. Check its modification time and row count:

```spl
| inputlookup asset_lookup_by_str
| eval _lookup_row=1
| stats count as rows
```

Compare the row count to the expected baseline; an unexpected drop is a sign the upstream export truncated.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.2.13: NIS2 evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Table Of Unknown Senders (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 2 — Choropleth If Geo Ip Enabled (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 3 — Link To Qualys Host Detail (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**Zero results when acceleration is fresh:** The field you're filtering on was likely added after the acceleration started — rebuild the acceleration.

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**NIS2-specific failure modes:**

• 24/7 reporting obligation (Art. 23): NIS2 requires early warning within 24 hours of awareness of a significant incident. Detections that back this SLA must compute elapsed time from the SOC's first-acknowledged timestamp (not the resolve-time) and must trigger independently of business hours — the regulatory clock does not pause overnight.

• Member-state variation: NIS2 is transposed individually by each EU Member State with some latitude on thresholds and sector scoping. A detection correct for German IT-SiG 3 (BSI) may be misaligned with Dutch CWV or French LPM. Use `nis2_member_state_config.csv` to parametrize thresholds per jurisdiction; never hardcode.

• Essential vs Important entity criteria: the applicable obligations depend on sector + size. Before alerting, verify the monitored entity's designation; a detection firing on an out-of-scope subsidiary causes unnecessary regulatory notifications that erode trust with the national CSIRT.

• Supply-chain (Art. 21(2)(d)) extent: NIS2 explicitly includes direct suppliers' cybersecurity posture. Detections must cover monitored-by-you-not-owned-by-you infrastructure (outsourced SaaS, managed SIEM tenants) — these are in scope even if operationally run by a third party. BA/BAA-style governance applies.
grandmaExplanation: We compare the address book of company computers with the real traffic on the internal roads. If something is sending lots of data but is not on the list, we point it out so the owners can register or shut it down.
